Dear Subscriber,
Issue No. 	2 • September 20 II
Entails That
Cause Wet Dreants
ut's get personal, baby.
If there's one thing you can do to stick out like an honest politician in "Vashington
DC in your prospects' in boxes, it's to show them the REAL you. Not the superstar you, or
the guru you, or even the expert you. But the REAL you-- the one your spouse or closest
friend only knows and that nobody else would ever know. Yes ... showing the real you in an
email can be a bit hair-raising. But the money it can generate will make it more than
worth it for you -- as you become the only authentic voice out of the hundreds of emails
they get each day (especially if they are on your competitors' lists). Let me give you a real
life example. As some of my subscribers know, I dabble in many niches, including a men's
health niche for guys who have prostate problems. Prostate problems have plagued my
family (dad, grandpa, uncle-- all have/had prostate cancer and prostate problems), and I
also suffered from symptoms briefly at the tender young age of 31. So I have a very
special ... connection ... to this particular market. On the next page is one of the emails I
sent to that list to show you what I mean by getting personal. I doubt anyone else in my
market has the ballz to do anything like this (or, if they did, they'd be raunchy or offensive
or obnoxious with it). Plus, it asks for the sale (and got the sale), which is the whole point
of writing the email in the first place. Anyway, here it is ...

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
Prostate Supplements That cause Wet Dreams
Hi,
Here's something interesting •••
Yesterday I 	started taking some new prostate supplements:
Pygeum & Saw Palmetto.
I 	am kind of a "skeptic" of saw palmetto -- although I 	realize some
people practically swear by it. 	But mostly, 	I 	don't think it does
as good a 	job at dealing with excess DHT as certain other herbs.
Especially the ancient Chinese herb I 	talk about in the 21 Day Prostate
Fix-- http://www.ProstateProblemsFixed.com/ebook -- eBook, 	in
chapter 1.
I'm not completely against Saw Palmetto.
But I 	think it's overrated.
As for Pygeum, 	I've been keen on testing that.
So yesterday, 	I 	took my first round of it in the early evening with
my dinner. 	Since supplements take a while to build up in your
system (it's good to always give prostate supplements 90 days -- at
least -- before pronouncing judgement either way) 	I 	did not expect
to feel anything.
But you know what happened?
I 	had a powerful orgasm in my sleep!
Yes, 	I 	speaketh of a wet dream.
I 	don't have those all that often.
And this could very well have been a fluke.
In fact, 	it's very likely it was 	just a fluke.
We'll see.
Anyway, 	just something to think about.
I'll be taking this pygeum & saw palmetto combo for at least
four months and will report back at the end of that time.

are on prescription drugs now.
Well, now you know a side of me you probably never thought you'd get to know!
But forget about that (plea'le ...) and let's talk about how you can write these kinds of
emails (starting TODAY) and profit from them. You see, I believe if you (yes, YOU, dear
Email Player) were to write a PERSONAL email like this showing your readers a private
side to your life ... and if you have a strong call to action (mine above isn't even that strong
yet still worked, but if I did it again I would have had a MUCH stronger call to action)
you would see an immediate bump in your profits. I mean, let's face it, there's just nobody
else doing this. It's different from anyone else in the inbox droning on about the same old,
same old.
So let's "psychoanalyze" this bad-boy so you can go forth and do the same ...
It's Got Entertainment Value
This is extremely important in emails. No, it's not slap-stick funny or anything like
that. But, for example, using old English when describing my dream ("yes, I speaketh of a
wet dream") has entertainment value. If you can tap into other languages (native or
foreign - Australian slang is perfect for this, IMHO...) to explain things, you automatically
get in peoples' psychology. It also told a true story (a short one). Stories are naturally
entertaining. People love stories and are "hardwired" to respond to them, communicate
using them, and absorb information better from them. Also, notice how even though it's
entertaining and personal, it's not crude. I know some people who would have taken this
too far and gotten too explicit. But I don't recommend doing that just for the sake of it.
Always treat your list with respect, and they'll be more open to buying from you.
-3-

-- 3 of 21 --

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
It Never Strays From
The Prospect's "World"
The entire email from top to bottom speaks to the market's pains and problems.
The whole thing is in their "world." This is extremely important because, as the world's
greatest negotiator Jim Camp says, "you are always safe in the other person's
world." That means, you are safe when talking about their pains and problems and
desires (even if in the context of a story about someone else). You cannot bore them. You
cannot tell them too much info. And your chances of making the sale go up dramatically.
It's Intimately Personal
Let's face it, the content of this email is something that'd normally be private. I
can't think of anyone else I'd tell something like this to besides maybe my wife. ("Of
course YOU were in the dream, honey... ") But I told this to a list of prostate
problem plagued strangers who have never met me. Many of them are on other lists
about prostate problems and I am fairly certain none of those other list owners are getting
this personal with them. This makes me instantly the most relatable guy. No, I'm not a
doctor or a urologist. But they know, like and trust me after reading emails like this -- and
that can make up for a lack of credentials. I've also let them inside to see they aren't alone
in their pain and am out there fighting the good fight for them testing and experimenting
with supplements. You can do the same in your emails. Always remember this truism
when writing em ails, "If you want to get paid, get perso1Ull•.. "
Asks For The Sale
Again, if there's a "chink" in this email's armor it's that I did not ask for the sale
aggressively enough. There is a nice curiosity pitch towards the beginning (where the link
is slipped in, mid-sentence). But the PS could have been better. Incidentally, you'll notice I
am harder on my own emails than I am other peoples' emails. I like to go back months
later and examine my emails and try to think of ways to improve them. It's a good habit
to get into. One day I'll recycle it and use it again and make even more sales due to this
routine. But aggressive or not, it made money. And I inserted the link in the email twice --
once towards the top and in the P.S. I always plug when it's relevant and makes sense, and
never just for the sake of it (unless I can't think of a place where it makes sense, then
sometimes you have no choice but to insert at the end or just put it in without context --
the only real email "rule" I follow is to plug something every time, no mater what).
-4-

-- 4 of 21 --

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
Left Them On A Clifthanger
. The emails contains a classic "cliffhanger" ending. This is something I'm looking
at domg more and more, and that I've been admittedly weak on up until recently.
Whenever you can future pace them a bit to looking forward to your next email, you
automatically increase the chances of that email being opened, clicked and bought from.
In this case, I told them I'd be using those supplements for 4 months and would report
back on what happens. A small thing that can add up to hig numbers over time.
Curiosity Subject Line
Curiosity is one of the best kinds of subject lines you can use. And in this case the
subject line is packed with the kind of curiosity anyone with a pulse in my market would
want to know. A lot of people with prostate problems aren't just suffering from urinary
problems. Many of them have zero sex drive, too. So again, the entire email (starting with
the subject line) is firmly entrenched in their "world." This is why market research is so
important. The better you know your market. the more money you 'II make.
Now it's your turn.
Time to get super personal with YOUR list.
Again, it can be a bit hair-raising. And at first (until you've done this a few times)
you may be wondering if you're pushing things too far. Maybe you are thinking of just
the right kind of personal information to reveal, but are afraid you're going to offend
someone. Hey, I can relate. But like most fears, this one is almost completely unfounded.
Frankly, I believe it SHOUW make you nervous to push that send button. That's how
you know you're on to something. And don't worry, chances are you're still not going too
far. You'll know you went too far when the market tells you. In the wet dream email
above, I didn't get one single complaint (which kind of disappointed me ...) which means I
probably fell far short of how far I mY!d have pushed it.
Anyway, here's the bottom line:
I believe if you were to write an email like this -- TODAY (why put it off?) to your
list, with as many of the above elements included as possible, then you would see an
immediate bump in your sales. And if you continue to do this sort of thing then (over

time) you will build a huge amount of trust your competition won't be able to touch with

register singing more than usual, too ...
The Art & 	Science
Of ''Ethically Fibbing''
Here's another example of being personal.
But in this sense, you're being personal by showing you're human and prone to
mistakes (even if the mistake in this case is on purpose). However, first, a disclaimer: Some
people might think the following is a bit sneaky. Maybe even "black hat." But really, there
is no victim and you will make your customer :MUCH happier and love you even more
than they do now. (And if that's a crime then slap the 'cuffs on my righteous arse and get
it over with!) Anyway, here's the scoop: Once upon a time while working for some clients,
we did an email-based sale using a similar launch process I use all the time now. But, I
made a dumb-o mistake. You see, I thought we were offering a 65% discount on the
product. But we weren't. It was actually more like a 92% discount! And it wasn't until the
last day of the sale when a customer pointed that out (I guess we all sucked at math!) So
we sent another email out saying, essentially, "you know what, I completely goofed!
I've been telling you how you get a 65% discount, but really it's 92°/o! Our
marketing guys are yelling at me about this now, but hopefully you
understand just how great this sale is that ends in the next 3 hours... "
Do you think that made us a few more sales?
You bet your gluteus assimus it did!
It really was a mistake though, in that case. However, that doesn't mean you can't
make this mistake on purpose. Again, nobody gets hurt (you 'II simply make them happy).
I know, I know! It's kinda fibbing, isn't it? Maybe technically it is. So if you have an ethical
reason for not doing this, I could not blame you. But I can tell you this, right now I'm in
the middle of a launch in another venture (female weight loss market) with a coupleJV
partners. And we're doing this on purpose. These lovely ladies think they're only getting a
50% discount when our product launches. But when launch day arrives, they'll be
pleasantly pleased to know the discount is really more like 64% off. It's purely
-6-
ww,v.BenSettle.com 	• 	Copyright 2011 Ben Settle, All Rights Reserved 	• 	Published By Settle, LLC
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 6 of 21 --

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
psychological and you get to use all kinds of interesting forces to your advantage. Like the
contrast theory (contrasting 65% with 92% in the case cited above). You are giving them
another reason to buy. You stand out from all the other 50% or "half off!" offers. And the
list goes on. This just works and works and works and works... (And even in the weight
loss thing above, it technically was true, we originally were only going to offer it for 50%
off. So we weren't even fibbing, we're just choosing to tell them they're getting an even
better deall.attr...)
Now a warning...
I would NOT recommend doing this more than once to the same list.
Yes, that's almost kind of a bummer, isn't it?
But think about it-- if you did this every single time, you'd eventually come off like
you're blatantly trying to manipulate and be gimmicky. Not saying that would make
anyone mad, necessarily (who would be mad that you're offering a bigger discount than
planned?) And maybe this is just my own personal hang-up on the matter. But gimmicks
that are obvious gimmicks tend to be more easily ignored and the offers not taken as
seriously. Personally, I think this is a great way to launch a~ product. If you want to see
a real life example of just how simple this can be to do via email, here's all I'm doing with
the current launch (hint: you can easily adapt this to your product...) --
Bi,
[PRODUCT NAME] 	is now ready!
Originally, 	I was going to let early bird buyers have it for 50%
off. But I 	changed my mind. 	I 	want to give you an even BIGGER
discount. 	So if you buy it before Sunday at midnight you can have
it for 64% off 	(for even more savings!)
You can download it here:
(URL)
(SIGNATURE)
P.S. 	Since not everyone gets their emails due to spam filters, 	etc,
I'll be sending short reminders about this tomorrow, 	Saturday and
Sunday, 	too.
-7-

Remember, 	the discount ends Sunday night:
(URL)
See?
Nothing fancy about it. Instead it's very simple (almost "matter-of-fact") so as to
not make it LOOK like a blatant gimmick of any kind. (It's sad but sometimes the really
good deals that are 100% legitimate are often the least trusted -- don't feed that skeptic
fire ...) By the way, as of the time I'm writing this newsletter issue, the above email has not
been sent yet. \Ve're in the middle of the pre-launch (it will launch next week). And I will
be showing you the emails for this particular launch in this newsletter in the coming
months (probably with product name and URL removed- a deal I made with theJV
partners, sorry!) What's important though is not the product name or URL or whatever.
It's the principles and ideas you can learn (and profit from) by seeing these emails in the
flesh. So stay tuned, and I'll let you see the entire 20+ email launch process -- all
displayed and psycho-analyzed for you.
En1ail Critique
Mushing along...
One thing I am going to be doing in these newsletters is email critiques of
members em ails. Remember, as a subscriber you get one free email critique
per month. If I charged for a service Jike this, I'd charge at LEAST $250 per email, but
you get one free each month. In some wa.ys it's like wffing $250 inside your
newsletter each month just for being a subscriber. Simply send your email to me
at ben@bensettle.com and I'll give you a thorough critique. Gust realize I reserve the right
to use your email and my critique as content for this newsletter so everyone can benefit.)

David this is a pretty good email. You said this is one of your first attempts and it's
a great one, no doubt about it. A little more practice and refinement and you'll be
awesome at this and stick out like a sore thumb in the affiliate marketing arena full of
carbon-copied emails. Some additional ideas to consider are: (l) Selling your own
product. I'm not anti-affiliate marketing, but when you get good at this, you'll make FAR
more of the green stuff selling your own product (or at the very least licensing someone
else's product so you get paid all the money) than you will selling someone else's as an
affiliate. In fact, there's no reason you can't do your own affiliate marketing product. This
would have been even better if you sold your own product that shows people how to do
affiliate marketing. You could have a chapter just on bonuses and you could use this email
to tease them into buying it. Sometimes I will write an email and then add the promise to
the product last minute based off the email I just wrote (if it's a digital product). It would
be as easy as saying something like:
uThere's a whole art and science to creating bonuses.
Almost everyone does it wrong, 	and loses out on a lot
of sales as a result. 	In my XYZ eBook I 	show you real
life examples of bonuses that are so powerful they make
the sale all by themselves, 	even if nobody wants your
main product ••• "
Anyway, that's just an example, but I think you get the idea. Another thing to think
about (and this goes for ALL of us) is you don't want to just plug in the PS necessarily.
Ideally, plug your product in the body of the email. And then again in the PS if you want.
But it's good to work the pitch into the body so it flows naturally and smoothly right from
the story and body content. Don't get me wrong... PS's are nice and I'm not saying not to
use them. But I've noticed people getting more "immune" to them online. It's almost like
they know that's where the pitch is and so they just skip it over. That's why you want to
vary it up and start the teasing and lead that into the plug. This is not always practical of
course. But just something to keep in mind. Your email could just as easily have ended

Ben Settle
